Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants convert light energy into chemical energy
stored in glucose. It occurs in the chloroplasts.

The light-dependent reactions take place in the thylakoid membranes and produce ATP and NADPH,
releasing oxygen as a by-product of splitting water.

The Calvin cycle (light-independent reactions) occurs in the stroma and fixes carbon dioxide into
glucose using the ATP and NADPH from the light reactions.

Chlorophyll a is the primary pigment that absorbs mostly blue and red light, reflecting green
light, which gives plants their color.
